title: The Defense Supercycle Is Here. RTX Has $271 Billion Reasons to Care.
subtitle: NATO spending hits $1.8 trillion. Patriot missiles are flying off the line. And RTX's backlog stretches three years deep. Earnings next week could light the fuse.
summary: RTX (RTX) is sitting on a record $271 billion backlog — that's over three years of locked-in revenue — as NATO defense spending surges past $1.8 trillion in 2026. With Patriot interceptor contracts from Germany and the Netherlands, a $6.6 billion F-35 engine deal, SPY-6 radar rollouts, and Stinger production doubling globally, RTX is the most diversified defense contractor in the world. Trading well below its highs with a forward P/E of 25.5x against 32.5% EPS growth, earnings on July 23 could be the catalyst that reminds the market this supercycle is just getting started.

videos.title: Why RTX Is Crushing Lockheed And Northrop!

bodyHtml:
There's a scene in every war movie where the general gets the call, stares at the map, and says some version of "we're going to need more ammo." That scene is playing out in real time across NATO capitals right now — and RTX is the company getting the phone call.

NATO defense spending just crossed $1.8 trillion in 2026. That's not a budget projection. That's cash that's been appropriated, signed, and is actively flowing into supply chains across the alliance. And the single biggest beneficiary of that tide? RTX — the $260 billion conglomerate that builds the missiles, the engines, and the avionics that every NATO country is desperately restocking.

Let's talk about that backlog. $271 billion. That's not a typo and it's not some soft pipeline number that analysts massage in earnings calls. That is signed contracts — firm orders for Patriot interceptors, F-35 engines, SPY-6 radars, AMRAAMs, Stingers, AIM-9X Sidewinders — that stretch more than three years into the future. In a world where most companies fight for 12 months of visibility, RTX has a 36-month head start. That's a moat most investors haven't even priced in.

And the contracts keep stacking. Germany just paid $3.7 billion for Patriot interceptors headed to Ukraine. The Netherlands dropped $627 million on Patriot systems. The Navy threw down $1.1 billion for AIM-9X Block II missiles. SPY-6 radar — the Navy's next-gen eyes and ears — landed a $515 million contract. Pratt & Whitney locked in $6.6 billion for F135 engines across Lots 18 and 19, plus a $3.8 billion modification on top. That's not a pipeline. That's a fire hose.

The financials back up the narrative. $90.4 billion in trailing twelve-month revenue, up nearly 9% year-over-year. Net income of $7.3 billion. Free cash flow of $7.2 billion — meaning RTX is printing cash while simultaneously investing $100 million into a new Rhode Island facility for radar testing and interceptor production. Operating margin sits at 13.2%. Profit margin at 8%. And EPS grew 32.5% year-over-year — a number that makes the current forward P/E of 25.5x look almost reasonable for a company growing earnings at that clip. At this multiple, you're paying roughly 25 bucks for every dollar of next year's earnings. For 32% growth, that's not expensive — that's pragmatic.

The stock has pulled back from its highs — like most of the defense sector has this year — but the fundamentals are accelerating, not decelerating. Trailing P/E of 36.4x is a little spicy, sure, but the forward multiple tells the real story. Analysts see it too: the Street's mean target sits around $215, with the high-end calling for $242. Fifteen buys, eight holds, zero sells. Institutional ownership at 81.4%. Smart money is already in, and they're not leaving because the thesis is still unfolding.

The bull case on RTX isn't complicated. It's the most diversified defense contractor on the planet. Raytheon does the missiles — Patriot, Stinger, Javelin, AMRAAM, Sidewinder — the stuff that wins headlines. Pratt & Whitney builds the engines — the F135 that powers every F-35, plus the Geared Turbofan that powers half the commercial fleet. Collins Aerospace handles the brains — avionics, communications, training systems. One company, three moats. If any one segment slows down, the other two are there to pick up the slack. That diversification is the reason RTX gets the call before anyone else.

And the supercycle has legs. NATO's $1.8 trillion isn't a one-year spike. Europe is building permanent production lines for Stinger missiles — production is literally doubling globally. AMRAAM manufacturing is expanding inside NATO countries. The UK just handed RTX a £2 billion Omnia Training contract to modernize the British Army's simulation and training infrastructure. These are multi-year, multi-decade commitments, not emergency stopgaps. Governments don't build permanent factories for one-year problems.

Next week is Q2 earnings — July 23 — and the consensus is calling for $1.66 EPS. Given the contract flow over the last six months, the whisper number is probably higher. If RTX beats and raises guidance — which it has the $271 billion backlog to justify — this stock could remind everyone why defense contractors compound like clockwork during supercycles. The setup is about as clean as it gets.

RTX is a quarter-trillion dollar company with a $271 billion backlog, accelerating earnings, and a geopolitical tailwind that shows no signs of easing. The stock's pulled back from its highs. The fundamentals haven't. That's the kind of disconnect that tends to correct itself. The only question is whether you're positioned for when it does.

Disclosure: The Signal holds no position in RTX. Positions may change. This is not financial advice.
